metadata.title: US Presidential Election Voting Data by Race and Political Party
metadata.description: Compiled from CNN National Exit Polls, Pew Research Center validated voter studies, and US Census Bureau Current Population Survey Voting and Registration Supplements.

notes.exit_polls: National Election Pool (NEP) exit polls conducted by Edison Research, published by CNN and other major networks. Percentages represent share of vote within each racial/ethnic group. Margins of error vary by subgroup size.
notes.census_data: US Census Bureau Current Population Survey (CPS) Voting and Registration Supplement. Turnout rates reflect self-reported voting among the citizen voting-age population. Registration rates reflect self-reported registration among the citizen voting-age population.
notes.other_category: 'Other' includes Native American, Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander, multiracial, and other racial/ethnic identifications not captured in the main categories. Exit poll and Census category definitions may differ slightly.
notes.pew_validated: Pew Research Center validated voter data uses commercial voter file matching to confirm turnout, producing different (typically higher) partisan loyalty estimates than exit polls for some subgroups.

sources: CNN National Exit Polls, 2016, 2020, 2024 (Edison Research / National Election Pool)
Pew Research Center, 'Behind Biden's 2020 Victory' (June 2021) and 'An Examination of the 2016 Electorate Based on Validated Voters' (August 2018)
US Census Bureau, Current Population Survey, Voting and Registration Supplement, November 2016 (P20-580), November 2020 (P20-585), November 2024 (P20-587)

census_turnout_and_registration.source: US Census Bureau CPS Voting and Registration Supplement, November 2016 (P20-580)
census_turnout_and_registration.note: Percentages are of citizen voting-age population (self-reported).

pew_validated_voter_data.note: Pew uses validated voter methodology matching survey responses to commercial voter files. Numbers may differ from exit polls due to methodology.

White.notes: Trump won White voters by 15 points. White voters were 71% of the electorate in Pew's validated sample.

Black.notes: Near-identical to 2016; highest Democratic loyalty of any racial group.

Hispanic.notes: Trump gained 10 points with Hispanic voters vs. 2018 midterms (25%→38%). Wide education gap: college-educated Hispanics 69% Biden, non-college 55% Biden.

census_turnout_and_registration.source: US Census Bureau CPS Voting and Registration Supplement, November 2020 (P20-585)
census_turnout_and_registration.note: Percentages are of citizen voting-age population (self-reported). 2020 saw the highest turnout in a presidential election since 1992.

census_turnout_and_registration.source: US Census Bureau CPS Voting and Registration Supplement, November 2024 (P20-587)
census_turnout_and_registration.note: Percentages are of citizen voting-age population (self-reported).

white_voters.trend: Consistently Republican-leaning. Trump won White voters by 21 points in 2016, 17 points in 2020, and 16 points in 2024. White share of electorate declined from 71% in 2016 to 67% in 2020, then returned to 71% in 2024.
white_voters.significance: White voters remain the largest voting bloc. The steady but modest decline in Republican margins (from +21 to +16) reflects gradual demographic and educational realignment. College-educated White voters have shifted toward Democrats, while non-college White voters remain strongly Republican.

black_voters.trend: Overwhelmingly Democratic. Black support for Democrats was 88% in 2016, 87% in 2020, and 85% in 2024. Republican share among Black voters rose from 8% (2016) to 13% (2024), with notable gains among Black men and younger Black voters.
black_voters.significance: Black voters remain the most reliably Democratic racial group, though the 2024 election showed a measurable shift, particularly among Black men. The 5-point decline in Democratic support from 2016 to 2024 was one factor in Harris's narrower margins.

hispanic_latino_voters.trend: Largest party-shift story of the three elections. Democratic support among Hispanic/Latino voters dropped from 65% in 2016 to 65% in 2020, then fell sharply to 52% in 2024. Republican share rose from 29% (2016) to 46% (2024) — a 17-point gain.
hispanic_latino_voters.significance: This is the most dramatic realignment in recent electoral history. The shift was especially pronounced among Hispanic men, working-class Hispanics, and in South Florida and South Texas. Hispanic voters can no longer be treated as a reliable Democratic constituency.

asian_voters.trend: Democratic support declined from 65% in 2016 to 54% in 2024. Republican share rose from 29% to 39% over the same period. Third-party support spiked to 7% in 2024, highest of any racial group.
asian_voters.significance: Asian American voters have been the fastest-growing racial/ethnic group in the electorate. The 2024 election showed significant movement toward Republicans and an unusually high third-party vote share.

other_voters.trend: Democratic-leaning but increasingly competitive. Democratic support held relatively steady (56% in 2016, 55% in 2020, 53% in 2024). Third-party support remains elevated relative to other groups.
other_voters.significance: This heterogeneous category (Native American, multiracial, Pacific Islander, etc.) shows diverse voting patterns but leans Democratic overall.

turnout.trend: Overall turnout rose from 61.4% (2016) to 66.8% (2020) — the highest since 1992 — then fell back to 63.9% in 2024. The 2020 spike was driven partly by expanded mail and early voting during the COVID-19 pandemic.
turnout.by_race: All groups saw turnout increases in 2020 and declines in 2024. White non-Hispanic voters consistently have the highest turnout. Hispanic turnout remains the lowest among major groups, though the gap has narrowed.
